description: Normative MUSTs a human reviewer confirms are present and correctly scoped in spec/v0.3.md. NOT executable.

asserts.assert: Returned `state` is declared UNTRUSTED and the agent MUST verify integrity it applied before use.
State-seal key MUST be per-agent, Hub-invisible, distinct from the callback credential, and never embedded in `state`.
`allowed_resolvers` absent defaults fail-closed (only the submitting agent may resolve).

A message's poll, callback, AND cancel access are bound to the submitting principal; a non-submitting agent MUST NOT poll, receive the callback for, or cancel a message by its `id` (`run_id` MUST NOT authorize cross-run/cross-principal access). Cancel is state-terminating, so the cancel endpoint is named explicitly.
Response integrity is required for ALL callback schemes via a detached signature over RFC 8785 JCS(signed_context).

The signed_context binds `payload_sha256` (v0.3) = lowercase-hex SHA-256 of JCS({response, state}), covering response.value/comment/actor/edited/resolved_at and state. The Hub signs over the delivered payload; the agent MUST RECOMPUTE the digest from the payload it received (never trusting a transmitted digest) and reject on mismatch. The top-level `agent`/`defaulted` are advisory; trust is derived from the bound `actor`/`resolution`.
Private/link-local/metadata refusal is applied at DELIVERY time (DNS-rebinding defense); no redirects; credential only for verified hosts.
All lifecycle transitions are atomic compare-and-set; first terminal wins and is immutable; answer at/before expires_at beats default_on_expire.
Hub durability (open messages, committed resolutions, delivered notifies, pending push obligations surviving restart) is a conformance MUST.

A Hub MUST reject a message whose `ma2h_version` MAJOR it does not recognize with `version_not_supported`. Per the v0.3 signature break, a Hub MUST also reject (or negotiate) a pre-0.3 message requesting a PUSH callback ‚Äî its signed Response is unverifiable by a pre-0.3 agent ‚Äî while PULL stays compatible (¬ß8.2).

Numeric payload values bound by `payload_sha256` are canonicalized per RFC 8785 ¬ß3.2.2.3 (the IEEE-754 double serialized as ECMAScript Number::toString ‚Äî ordinary decimals included). Implementations MUST use a conformant JCS impl (non-JS ‚Üí a vetted library) and MUST preserve strings as-is (RFC 8785 ¬ß3.1 does NOT normalize Unicode). Exactness caveat: an integer beyond ¬±(2^53-1) WHOSE EXACT VALUE MUST BE PRESERVED MUST be carried as a string ‚Äî a bare JSON number still canonicalizes deterministically (dp-004 includes `1e21` -> `1e+21`) but rounds to the nearest double. The dp-004 vector pins the canonical bytes + digest.

A v0.4 directive is authored by a Hub-attested `from` (`^(human|system):.+$`, derived from the operator session ‚Äî never request-supplied) and addressed `to` an `agent:<id>`. It carries no `request`/`action`/`state` (schema-enforced): inbound ask/task are deferred and a directive is the one-way notify equivalent.

The inbox drain (GET /v1/inbox) and ack (POST /v1/inbox/ack) are authenticated by the agent's bearer credential scoped to `agent.id`, which selects the mailbox; a principal MUST NOT drain or ack another agent's mailbox, and a foreign/unknown directive id MUST be indistinguishable from a non-existent one.

Directive delivery is at-least-once with explicit consume/ack and FIFO-per-agent on first delivery; an unacked directive is redelivered after the visibility window and retained until acked or expired; the optional webhook reuses the ¬ß8.3 retry rules and ¬ß9.4 SSRF controls, with the mailbox as the source of truth.

The Hub MUST sign EVERY directive delivery with a detached signature over RFC 8785 JCS(inbound_signed_context) = { from, id, jti, ma2h_version, payload_sha256, t, to }, RE-SIGNED per delivery with a fresh t/jti (so an old mailbox directive stays in-window). `payload_sha256` = lowercase-hex SHA-256 of JCS({directive:<title/body/priority/tags/context present>}); the agent MUST recompute it from the received directive, verify, reject a t outside the window and a replayed jti (jti cache TTL >= window), and dedup on the directive `id`. Verification is a MUST on BOTH channels (pull mailbox included) because a directive is an unsolicited inbound command. `to` binds against cross-agent replay. created_at/expires_at/sensitive are UNBOUND advisory metadata (Hub-authoritative) and MUST NOT drive agent security decisions.

The agent MUST also validate the received directive's SHAPE against inbound-message.schema.json ‚Äî payload_sha256 binds only the content fields, so a forbidden request/action/state injected in transit keeps a valid signature and MUST be rejected. After verifying, the agent MUST confirm `to` addresses ITSELF and reject otherwise ‚Äî the signature binds `to`, but only the recipient's own-identity check stops a directive validly signed for agent:X being acted on by agent:Y (the webhook channel has no Hub-side mailbox gate). The safe ordering is verify -> validate shape -> confirm addressee -> act durably/idempotently -> record id -> ack; id-dedup is at-most-once within the dedup horizon, so cross-restart at-most-once needs an idempotent action or persisted dedup state.

v0.4 is additive and non-breaking: it changes no v0.3 wire format, a 0.4 Hub accepts 0.3 agent->human envelopes, and the push-parity threshold is anchored at the signature-break minor (3), so a 0.4 Hub still accepts a 0.3 push and still rejects a pre-0.3 push.
Hub durability extends to the inbound leg: an unacked directive in a mailbox and a pending directive-webhook obligation MUST survive a Hub restart (drainable until acked or expired).

The acknowledgment/receipt is one terminal artifact modeled once: a shared `ack` envelope (Hub-attested `by`; `in_reply_to` the directive or ask/task id) + an `acknowledged` status on a delivery track ORTHOGONAL to the ¬ß7 `resolution`. Directive: queued‚Üídelivered‚Üíacknowledged (the receipt folds into the ¬ß8.7 mailbox consume via an optional `note`); ask/task: answered‚Üídelivered-to-agent‚Üíacknowledged. First-ack-wins (terminal, immutable). The response-leg ack (`POST /v1/messages/{id}/ack`) is submitter-bound and requires a terminal message.

An ack is informational to the human, so its trust mirrors the ¬ß6 response leg, NOT the ¬ß9.7 directive rule: PULL is trusted via the authenticated Hub GET (unsigned); a Hub that PUSHES an ack to a human callback MUST sign it with a detached signature over ack_signed_context = { ack_sha256, by, in_reply_to, jti, ma2h_version, t }. ack_sha256 is SHA-256 of JCS of a FIXED-KEY object { acked_at, by, in_reply_to, note, resolution_id } with each absent optional (note/resolution_id) serialized as JSON null (as ¬ß9.2's { response, state } wrapper does), so the digest is unambiguous regardless of which optionals are present; the client recomputes ack_sha256 from the received ack using this same fixed-key/null wrapper and rejects a replayed jti / out-of-window t. The Hub attests `by` and stamps the AUTHORITATIVE resolution_id (never a client-supplied one). The agent-authored `note` is untrusted agent‚Üíhuman text, ¬ß9.6-sanitized.

Presence is DERIVED from the agent's existing authenticated poll/long-poll/SSE activity (a per-agent `last_seen`) ‚Äî no heartbeat, no always-on connection. States by an advertised freshness window: `online` (fresh) / `offline` (stale) / `unknown` (never seen, distinct from offline). Read at GET /v1/agents/{id}/presence, OWNER-ONLY per-account ‚Äî a non-owned/unknown agent reads `unknown`, never leaking activity timing.
